result.chapter_summary: Educational failures in the U.S. and France are not due to a lack of resources but are the result of 'faulty ideas' prioritizing child-centered individualism over communal knowledge. Hirsch argues that the achievement gap is primarily a knowledge gap that can only be closed by shifting from skill-based, individualized instruction to a uniform, knowledge-rich curriculum.

claims.text: Six major educational frustrations in the United States—over-testing, preschool fade-out, narrowed curriculum, low high school verbal scores, stagnant achievement gaps, and Common Core struggles—are caused by the reign of faulty ideas.
claims.source_passage: These problems have defied solution—but not primarily from lack of will or money, or from poverty or the shortcomings of teachers. They resist solution because they cannot be solved under the reign of the faulty ideas that caused them to arise.

claims.text: Changing underlying educational ideas is the most effective form of educational reform.
claims.source_passage: My long-standing view is that idea change will be the most effective educational reform of all.

claims.text: The achievement gap between social groups is primarily a knowledge gap and a language gap rather than a result of genetics or sociology.
claims.source_passage: Against the tide of sociological and genetic explanations of achievement gaps, the path-breaking work of researchers like John Guthrie, Betty Hart, and Todd Risley should have made it unnecessary to assert once more at this late date that the achievement gap is chiefly a knowledge gap and a language gap.

claims.text: Knowledge-based schooling can significantly ameliorate the achievement gap.
claims.source_passage: It can be greatly ameliorated by knowledge-based schooling.

claims.text: Parity of knowledge among young pupils should be understood as a civil right.
claims.source_passage: Once the centrality of knowledge (not general “skills”) is fully grasped by educators and the wider public, the right to parity of knowledge among young pupils will come to be understood as a civil right.

claims.text: Imparting the knowledge possessed by successful citizens is the only way to provide all children with the possibility of success.
claims.source_passage: This book continues my earlier theme that only by systematically imparting to all children the knowledge that is commonly possessed by successful citizens can all children gain the possibility of success

claims.text: Success in education should be defined as becoming an autonomous person who can communicate effectively with strangers, earn a living, and contribute to the community.
claims.source_passage: “success” understood as becoming a person with autonomy, who commands respect, has a communal voice that can write and speak effectively to strangers, can earn a good living, and can contribute to the wider community.

claims.text: Elementary schools must shift their primary goal from child-centered self-realization to community-centeredness.
claims.source_passage: The book has benefited from my clearer understanding that the key task facing our elementary schools is to shift our emphasis from the goal of self-realization to the goal of community—from child-centeredness to community-centeredness.

claims.text: The French elementary school system was historically characterized by an egalitarian uniformity where all children studied the same national curriculum.
claims.source_passage: For many decades the French elementary school had been the pride and the terror of the young, with every child, rich or poor, having to undergo the very same rigors under the same national curriculum.

claims.text: The 1989 French education law (loi Jospin) replaced the uniform national curriculum with locally determined, individualized curriculums.
claims.source_passage: But in 1989, the bicentenary of the French Revolution, France passed a radical new education law—the loi Jospin—requiring all elementary schools to cease teaching the national curriculum and begin teaching locally determined curriculums, individualized further by a special emphasis in each school, called its projet.

claims.text: The 1989 French educational reform was prepared for by two decades of 'indoctrination' in American-style progressive education within French teacher training schools.
claims.source_passage: This drastic change had been silently prepared for during two decades of teacher indoctrination within French education schools into American-style progressive education.

claims.text: The 1989 French reforms prioritized general skills like 'critical thinking' over specific subject-matter knowledge.
claims.source_passage: To compensate for all this novel heterogeneity, the unifying emphasis was to be on general skills such as “critical thinking” and “learning to learn.”

claims.text: In 1989, France passed the loi Jospin, which required elementary schools to replace the national curriculum with locally determined, individualized curricula.
claims.source_passage: But in 1989, the bicentenary of the French Revolution, France passed a radical new education law—the loi Jospin—requiring all elementary schools to cease teaching the national curriculum and begin teaching locally determined curriculums, individualized further by a special emphasis in each school, called its projet.

claims.text: The French shift to individualized curricula aimed to prioritize student individuality, native abilities, interests, and home culture.
claims.source_passage: The new law reflected those ideas: more attention was to be paid to the individuality of each student, to his or her native abilities, interests, and home culture.

claims.text: In response to increased curricular heterogeneity, the French system shifted its unifying focus to general skills like 'critical thinking' and 'learning to learn.'
claims.source_passage: To compensate for all this novel heterogeneity, the unifying emphasis was to be on general skills such as “critical thinking” and “learning to learn.”

claims.text: The 1989 French educational reform constituted a complete 'Americanization' of the French school system.
claims.source_passage: In other words, in 1989 the French decided to completely Americanize their school system overnight.

claims.text: The implementation of the loi Jospin created a valid natural experiment comparing communal, knowledge-centered schooling with child-centered, skill-centered schooling.
claims.source_passage: The sudden organizational change introduced by the new loi Jospin instituted a vast natural experiment. Which mode of schooling would work better and more fairly: the community-centered and knowledge-centered mode of the past, or the child-centered and skills-centered mode of the future?

claims.text: Key factors of French education—including teacher quality, budgets, and preschools—remained constant during the post-1989 curricular shift.
claims.source_passage: Teacher quality stayed the same by objective measures. School buildings and budgets did not change significantly. The superb French preschools were not covered by the new law, and stayed essentially the same.

claims.text: The primary cause of changes in French educational outcomes after 1989 was the change in curriculum and pedagogy.
claims.source_passage: The most decisive change was in the curriculum and pedagogy of the elementary school.

claims.text: Every demographic group in France experienced a steep decline in achievement following the 1989 educational reforms.
claims.source_passage: They reported an astonishingly steep decline in achievement in each demographic group—children from the homes of wealthy executives and professionals, children from the middle classes, children from various other well-­defined demographic groups including the unemployed.

claims.text: The post-1989 French educational system exacerbated social inequality, harming the most economically disadvantaged children the most.
claims.source_passage: Each group was academically harmed by the new system, and the harm became ever greater as one went down the economic scale. The children of the unemployed declined most of all. Achievement decreased. Inequality increased dramatically.

claims.text: The decline in French test scores among children of elite professionals proves that immigration is not the cause of the general educational decline in France.
claims.source_passage: The massive declines that occurred at the very top among children of white-collar workers and high-level professionals and executives cannot be blamed on the influx of North African immigrants, as some American experts are inclined to say.

claims.text: American education experts tend to blame immigrants for the French decline because they lack detailed knowledge of the French data.
claims.source_passage: Why are American education experts inclined to blame immigrants for the French decline? They know little about the details.

claims.text: French longitudinal studies provide a level of statistical confidence comparable to the American NAEP.
claims.source_passage: By those numbers alone, even without the refinements introduced by French and American experts, the two samplings yield very similar levels of confidence.

claims.text: Educational outcomes are primarily controlled by the dominance of specific ideas rather than by irresistible social forces.
claims.source_passage: These historical facts, coupled with recent cognitive research, will add credibility to the view that our educational fate is largely controlled by ideas rather than by irresistible social forces.

claims.text: The French educational decline after 1989 was a duplication of the American decline of the 1960s and 1970s, caused by a change in guiding ideas.
claims.source_passage: After 1989, the French in effect duplicated the American decline of the 1960s and 1970s by means of the same basic change in guiding ideas.

claims.text: American academic scores at all grade levels fell by more than 25 percent of a standard deviation between 1960 and 1980.
claims.source_passage: But between 1960 and 1980 American academic scores fell rapidly at all grade levels—more than 25 percent of a standard deviation, a big drop for large populations.

claims.text: The chief cause of the American educational decline was the nationwide adoption of inadequate ideas favoring individualistic development over communal education.
claims.source_passage: The chief cause of the decline was the nationwide adoption of a set of inadequate ideas. Though the ideas were partly true and beneficial, they were also partly incorrect and harmful because they neglected the communal dimension of education in favor of individualistic child-centered development.

claims.text: Early education should be appropriate to the child's age and nature as part of a natural developmental process.
claims.source_passage: Early education should be appropriate to the child’s age and nature, as part of a natural developmental process.

claims.text: Early education should be individualized to follow the learning styles and interests of each student.
claims.source_passage: Early education should be individualized as far as possible—to follow the learning styles and interests of each developing student.

claims.text: The unifying aim of education is to develop critical thinking and other general skills.
claims.source_passage: The unifying aim of education is to develop critical thinking and other general skills.

claims.text: Early education should be chiefly communal, focused on gaining proficiency in the language and conventions of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: Early education should be chiefly communal—focused on gaining proficiency in the language and the conventions of the public sphere.

claims.text: Every child in a given locality should study basically the same early curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Every child in each locality should study basically the same early curriculum.

claims.text: The unifying aim of early schooling is to impart to every child the enabling knowledge possessed by the most successful adults in the wider society.
claims.source_passage: The unifying aim of early schooling is autonomy and equality of opportunity: to impart to every child the enabling knowledge that is possessed by the most successful adults in the wider society.

claims.text: If a piece of knowledge is not inherently harmful for an advantaged child to know, it cannot be developmentally inappropriate for a disadvantaged child.
claims.source_passage: if an advantaged child at age seven knows certain things without harm, then it cannot be inherently harmful or "developmentally inappropriate" for a disadvantaged child also to know those very same things at the same age.

claims.text: Elementary school is a time for building socialization, which is the only means through which individuality can ultimately express itself.
claims.source_passage: Elementary school is a time for building socialization as the only means through which individuality can ultimately express itself.

claims.text: True individuality is achieved only through full membership in the 'tribe' via shared knowledge and conventions.
claims.source_passage: Only full membership in the tribe leads to individuality, as G. H. Mead profoundly observed.

claims.text: A communalist curriculum accommodates shared topics for each child, whereas a child-centered school devises different curriculum topics for different children.
claims.source_passage: And the communalist will further caution that there is a big distinction between accommodating shared curriculum topics for each child as the best schools do in the community-centered schools of Finland and Japan, and devising different curriculum topics for different children as we and the French now do in the child-centered school.

claims.text: Children must master shared conventions of standard language and social interaction to achieve full membership in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: Children need to master the shared conventions of the standard language and of social interaction. They need to learn the shared knowledge and vocabulary of the nation, the shared spelling, pronunciation, and other conventions in the public sphere of the grown-up world.

claims.text: Only full membership in the social 'tribe' leads to the development of individuality.
claims.source_passage: Only full membership in the tribe leads to individuality, as G. H. Mead profoundly observed.

claims.text: Naturalistic and individualistic educational views have paradoxically turned schools into soulless test-prep factories.
claims.source_passage: But I will show in chapter 1 that, paradoxically, it is the naturalistic and individualistic view that has turned schools into soulless test-prep factories, with endless practice of strategies and skills, as they desperately attempt to overcome children’s lack of enabling knowledge—a lack partly induced by an individualized rather than a communal curriculum.

claims.text: Current educational assumptions lead to instruction that is not actually child-centered and perpetuates social inequality.
claims.source_passage: On the contrary, our assumptions about how children learn have led to instruction that is far from child centered, and that perpetuates inequality among children from different backgrounds.

claims.text: Every child in a democracy deserves access to the shared, enabling knowledge and language of the de facto public commons.
claims.source_passage: The Core Knowledge Sequence has always been offered as just one exemplification of the more general idea that there exists a de facto public commons that enables our national language to be deployed effectively, and that every child in a democracy should have access to that shared, enabling knowledge and language.

claims.text: The general communal principle in education can be realized by different curricula that vary in their specific content.
claims.source_passage: The main mission of the Foundation is to serve that general communal idea, which can be realized by different curricula that vary in interesting ways.

claims.text: From 1975 to 1985, France possessed the highest achieving and most egalitarian school system of any large country in Europe.
claims.source_passage: No large nation has done so more successfully than France did from 1975 to 1985, when it had the highest achieving, most egalitarian school system of any large country in Europe.

claims.text: Earlier American school districts possessed a strong communal purposiveness before the shift in educational theory.
claims.source_passage: The Americans, of course, never had a national curriculum like the French, but the schools of most American districts did in earlier days have a strong communal purposiveness.

claims.text: Skill-centrism—the aim of imparting content-independent general skills—is a problematic and invalid educational goal.
claims.source_passage: But the third guiding idea, which one could call skill-centrism—the aim of imparting critical-thinking skills and similar general skills like problem solving—is altogether problematic.

claims.text: Naturalism and individualism in education stem from a theological belief that nature is a manifestation of a benevolent God and is therefore unerring.
claims.source_passage: Naturalism and individualism go together. They arose from a belief that nature, as the earthly manifestation of a beneficent God, is unerring and benign.

claims.text: The phrase 'providential individualism' describes the faith that letting educational affairs take a natural course will lead to optimal results.
claims.source_passage: I have adopted the phrase providential individualism to capture this point of view. I have found it useful in describing the widely held faith that if we let affairs take their natural course we are in the hands of a benign Providence, so all will be well, even optimal, in education.

claims.text: Empathetic and loving pedagogy is the best approach for young children, but it is not the exclusive property of naturalism or individualism.
claims.source_passage: Of course, naturalism and individualism have no monopoly on a loving and empathetic teaching, which is in all cases the best pedagogy for young children.

claims.text: The belief that early curriculum must be tailored to a child's individual abilities and temperament is a fatal weakness of American education.
claims.source_passage: Naturalism and individualism... have also been taken to imply—and this is a fatal weakness—a curriculum that arises from the child’s individual abilities and temperament.

claims.text: Equating early education with the metaphor of individual 'development' is misleading and unsupported by developmental psychology.
claims.source_passage: I will argue, with support from developmental psychology, that equating early education with the metaphor of individual “development” is misleading... To make the emphases and content of the child’s early schooling largely dependent upon the child’s uniqueness is an idea unsupported by developmental psychology.

claims.text: Social impositions in schooling are natural occurrences despite being labeled 'unnatural' by developmentalists.
claims.source_passage: so-called “unnatural” social impositions are the most natural things in the world

claims.text: School systems with 'lockstep' or uniform curricula, such as Finland and Japan, produce effective results and happy students.
claims.source_passage: school systems with so-called “lockstep” curricula in the early grades (Finland, Japan) have very child-happy effective schools that score near the top in international studies.

claims.text: A differentiated curriculum is harmful to student achievement and educational equity.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, international studies have shown that a differentiated curriculum is harmful to achievement and equity.

claims.text: There is weak to nonexistent scientific evidence supporting the existence of individual 'learning styles.'
claims.source_passage: The evidence for individual learning styles is weak to nonexistent.

claims.text: Individualizing the elementary classroom inevitably leads to the fragmentation of the school curriculum.
claims.source_passage: And in practice the individualizing of the elementary-school classroom has led to fragmentation of the curriculum.

claims.text: The doctrine of 'general skills' (like critical thinking) is used as a defense for fragmented and individualized curricula.
claims.source_passage: This fragmentation is defended and supposedly turned to benefit by a third doctrine: that the goal of education is the imparting of general skills like critical thinking, creative thinking, problem solving, and cooperative thinking.

claims.text: Educational individualism logically requires the existence of general, content-independent skills to maintain educational unity.
claims.source_passage: Educational individualism has always required the general-skills idea. To make thinking skill the ultimate goal renders irrelevant the fragmenting of school topics that must occur when the teacher is urged to tailor the curriculum to the uniqueness of each child.

claims.text: Modern cognitive science has refuted the idea that students can be trained in content-independent thinking skills.
claims.source_passage: But reality has not accepted this hopeful idea about skills, and recent cognitive science has been fatal to it.

claims.text: John Dewey proposed that scientific 'habits of thought' could serve as a unifying principle to prevent individualized schooling from becoming mere distraction.
claims.source_passage: Dewey is right about the structure of the difficulty... Dewey says, by making critical thinking rather than mere facts the proper goal of child-centered education.

claims.text: Few people in American education are willing to challenge the prevailing emphasis on individualism due to its political sensitivity.
claims.source_passage: he has also identified what may be the most recalcitrant political problem in American education—that few dare challenge our emphasis on individualism.

claims.text: The term 'expertise' is more accurate than 'skills' because expertise inherently fuses knowledge and formal ability.
claims.source_passage: It is a far more accurate term than skills because expertise implies an inherent fusion of form and content, knowledge and skill that can’t easily be dissociated in the human mind.

claims.text: Broad subject-matter knowledge serves as the foundation for problem-solving abilities.
claims.source_passage: broad subject-matter knowledge is the basis of problem-solving abilities.

claims.text: If instruction is individualized 'in mass,' a further principle is required to guide instruction and provide unity to the student's experience.
claims.source_passage: if schooling is to be truly individualized and the students are no longer to be taught 'in mass,' then some further principle is needed to guide instruction and lend unity to the experience of the individual student.

claims.text: Critical thinking, rather than facts, is the principle Dewey proposed to unify child-centered education.
claims.source_passage: That can only be accomplished, Dewey says, by making critical thinking rather than mere facts the proper goal of child-centered education.

claims.text: The refusal to challenge individualism is one of the most persistent political problems in American education.
claims.source_passage: he has also identified what may be the most recalcitrant political problem in American education—that few dare challenge our emphasis on individualism.

claims.text: The idea that critical thinking can unify individualized schooling was based on scientifically incorrect views of skill development.
claims.source_passage: The proposal that critical thinking is an aim that unifies fragmented and individualized schooling made sense in Dewey’s era, when scientists had incorrect ideas about skill development.

claims.text: General cognitive abilities such as learning and reasoning cannot be studied or developed independently of specific content domains.
claims.source_passage: Research clearly rejects the classical views on human cognition in which general abilities such as learning, reasoning, problem solving, and concept formation correspond to capacities and abilities that can be studied independently of the content domains.

claims.text: Skills are intrinsically tied to particular content domains, a principle known as domain specificity.
claims.source_passage: Modern cognitive psychology holds that the skills that are to be imparted to a child by the school are intrinsically tied to particular content domains. This is called the domain specificity of skills.

claims.text: Thinking skills cannot be easily transferred from one subject matter to another.
claims.source_passage: Thinking skills cannot readily be separated from one subject matter and applied to other subject matters.

claims.text: The term 'expertise' is a more accurate description of educational goals than 'skill' because it implies the necessity of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Think of how significantly our view of schooling might change if suddenly policy makers, instead of using the term skill, had to use the more accurate, knowledge-drenched term expertise.

claims.text: A single, overarching general skill does not exist.
claims.source_passage: Yet that single, overarching skill doesn’t exist. Believing in that mirage has actually resulted in the 'distraction' Dewey feared.

claims.text: The combination of child-centeredness and the faulty theory of general skills has led to an education system dominated by soul-deadening drills.
claims.source_passage: A benign child-centeredness coupled with a faulty theory about general skills has led us to a child purgatory of skill drills.

claims.text: Skill drills have failed to improve both actual skills and standardized test scores.
claims.source_passage: These have produced neither good skills nor good scores on the ever-looming tests.

claims.text: No sensible person would disparage either the goal of child-centered self-realization or the goal of community-centeredness.
claims.source_passage: No sensible person would disparage either goal. But the emphasis must shift decisively for the sake of the community and the individual child.

claims.text: The verbal scores of American seventeen-year-olds have stayed low and largely unchanged since the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) began recording them in 1971.
claims.source_passage: The verbal scores of our seventeen-year-olds have stayed low and largely unchanged ever since NAEP began recording them in 1971.

claims.text: The education gap between Black and White students in the United States narrowed steadily until recent decades.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, as John Bishop has long pointed out, the education gap between blacks and whites had narrowed steadily until recent decades.

claims.text: While educational historians correctly reject the notion of a 'golden age,' they concede that a massive test score decline occurred between 1960 and 1980.
claims.source_passage: The belief that there was once a golden age of American education is scorned by educational historians. They are of course right. But they concede that there was indeed a large test score decline—over a quarter of a standard deviation—in grade school and high school test scores between 1960 and 1980.

claims.text: If a piece of knowledge is not inherently harmful for an advantaged seven-year-old to know, it cannot be logically classified as 'developmentally inappropriate' for a disadvantaged child of the same age.
claims.source_passage: foes of inequality like me caution that if an advantaged child at age seven knows certain things without harm, then it cannot be inherently harmful or 'developmentally inappropriate' for a disadvantaged child also to know those very same things at the same age.

claims.text: Naturalists in education concede that communal knowledge is important, just as communalists concede that natural development cannot be thwarted.
claims.source_passage: Naturalists will of course concede that communal knowledge is important, while communalists will concede that nature cannot be thwarted.

claims.text: The primary objective of this book is to promote the general communal principle of education rather than to advocate for any specific curriculum product like the Core Knowledge Sequence.
claims.source_passage: But the promotion of any single curriculum guide has been far from my mind, and is not a motivation for this book. Rather, this book's aim is to promote the general communal principle.

claims.text: The core of educational 'naturalism' and 'individualism' is the theological assumption that a benevolent purpose is inherent in Nature and will guarantee optimal outcomes if left to take its course.
claims.source_passage: The source of this faith is the unspoken assumption that a benevolent purpose is present in Nature, and will assure a beneficent result.

claims.text: The adoption of critical thinking as an educational goal was a practical necessity intended to provide unity to a curriculum that had been fragmented by individualized instruction.
claims.source_passage: He is stating a practical necessity: if the content of the curriculum is to be scattered and diversified by 'dealing with pupils individually and not in mass,' then some further principle is needed to guide instruction and lend unity to the experience of the individual student. That can only be accomplished, Dewey says, by making critical thinking rather than mere facts the proper goal of child-centered education.

claims.text: The French longitudinal studies (based on 4,200 students from a cohort of 1 million) yield statistical confidence levels similar to the American NAEP (based on 8,700 students from 6 million).
claims.source_passage: The French longitudinal studies are based on a sample size of forty-two hundred students from an age cohort of one million. By those numbers alone... the two samplings yield very similar levels of confidence.

evidence.description: Research identifying the achievement gap as a knowledge and language gap.
evidence.source_reference: John Guthrie, Betty Hart, and Todd Risley

evidence.description: A 1790 pamphlet advocating for common education for children to support egalitarianism.
evidence.source_reference: Condorcet, A Common Education for Children (1790)

evidence.description: Historical letter urging teachers to impart knowledge common and indispensable to all.
Public statement asserting that identical knowledge for all children reduces inequality.
Radical shift in French law moving from national to local/individualized curriculum.
French Ministry of Education longitudinal studies comparing 10-year-olds' achievement before and after 1989.
evidence.source_reference: French Ministry of Education researchers (cited in Endnote 7)
evidence.source_passage: The broad new law enabled the Ministry of Education to conduct longitudinal studies comparing the effects of the communal elementary curriculum before 1989 with those of the individualized, skill-centered curriculums that followed.

evidence.description: Comparison of sample sizes between French longitudinal studies (4,200/1M) and NAEP (8,700/6M).
evidence.source_reference: NAEP and French Ministry studies (cited in Endnote 11)
evidence.source_passage: The French longitudinal studies are based on a sample size of forty-two hundred students from an age cohort of one million... the two samplings yield very similar levels of confidence.

evidence.description: Objective measures of French teacher quality, budgets, and school buildings remaining constant over time.
evidence.source_reference: Positions du grip sur la crise de l'école (Endnote 8)
evidence.source_passage: Teacher quality stayed the same by objective measures. School buildings and budgets did not change significantly.

evidence.description: NAEP records showing verbal scores for 17-year-olds have remained low and flat since 1971.
evidence.source_passage: The verbal scores of our seventeen-year-olds have stayed low and largely unchanged ever since NAEP began recording them in 1971.

evidence.description: Research showing the narrowing of the black-white education gap until recent decades.
evidence.source_reference: John Bishop, 'Is the Test Score Decline Responsible for the Productivity Growth Decline?' (1989)
evidence.source_passage: Moreover, as John Bishop has long pointed out, the education gap between blacks and whites had narrowed steadily until recent decades.

evidence.description: Cross-national studies of science achievement showing US performance in the mid-20th century.
evidence.source_reference: J. P. Keeves, 'Learning Science in a Changing World' (1992)
evidence.source_passage: As late as the 1940s and 1950s the public education of the United States, for all its racial and social shortcomings, scored near the top among nations in both achievement and equality.

evidence.description: Historical reporting on the 'A Nation at Risk' commission following the 1970s score decline.
evidence.source_reference: National Commission on Excellence in Education, 'A Nation at Risk' (1983)
evidence.source_passage: Those puzzling disasters caused the Reagan administration to convene a national commission that produced the famous alarmist report A Nation at Risk (1983).

evidence.description: Comparison of community-centered schools in Finland and Japan vs. child-centered schools in the US and France.
The Core Knowledge Sequence (1990) as a functional exemplification of communal knowledge.
French student performance data from 1975-1985 showing high achievement and equality.
The historical presence of common historical and cultural vocabulary in early grade curricula in American districts prior to the 'expanding environments' model.
PISA 2012 results showing American students below nations with communal curricula in problem-solving abilities.
International assessment data showing that differentiated curricula and tracking are negatively associated with student performance.
evidence.source_reference: Elliott A. Medrich, International Mathematics and Science Assessment: What Have We Learned?, NCES 92-011, 1992
evidence.source_passage: Use of a differentiated curriculum based on tracking is negatively associated with student performance on the international assessments and also reduces opportunities for some students to be exposed to more advanced curriculum.

evidence.description: The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance, summarizing findings that refutes the concept of content-independent general skills.
evidence.source_reference: K. Anders Ericsson et al., The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance (2006)
evidence.source_passage: research on thinking skills is now a well-developed field, and its findings are fatal to this crucial refuge of current educational theory.

evidence.description: A review of cognitive science literature showing that evidence for the existence of learning styles (Visual, Auditory, Kinesthetic) is non-existent.
evidence.source_reference: Daniel Willingham, 'Ask the Cognitive Scientist,' American Educator (2005)
evidence.source_passage: The evidence for individual learning styles is weak to nonexistent.

evidence.description: A comprehensive summary of research in cognitive psychology rejecting the idea of general abilities independent of content.
evidence.source_reference: Paul J. Feltovich, Michael J. Prietula, and K. Anders Ericsson, 'Studies of Expertise from Psychological Perspectives,' in The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance (2006)
evidence.source_passage: Research clearly rejects the classical views on human cognition in which general abilities such as learning, reasoning, problem solving, and concept formation correspond to capacities and abilities that can be studied independently of the content domains.

evidence.description: Asserts that developing a general skill is a scientific impossibility.
evidence.source_reference: Anders Ericsson and Robert Pool, Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise (2016)
evidence.source_passage: There is no such thing as developing a general skill.

evidence.description: PISA scores showing that countries with communal, content-rich curricula outperform the US in creative problem-solving.
evidence.source_reference: PISA 2012 Results: Creative Problem Solving Students’ Skills in Tackling Real-Life Problems (2014)
evidence.source_passage: The most recent PISA scores for the problem-solving abilities of fifteen-year-olds place American students well below Korea, Japan, Canada, and Finland, nations with set, communal curricula in early grades

concepts.author_definition: The true nature of the achievement gap, where disparities in academic performance are actually disparities in the specific knowledge and language children possess.
An educational goal focused on the common knowledge and communal voice required for a child to participate in the wider public sphere.
A specific, locally determined emphasis in French schools introduced by the 1989 law to individualize the curriculum.
A 1989 French education law that replaced the national curriculum with locally determined, individualized curriculums focused on student-centered pedagogy.
A research scenario where a sudden organizational change allows for the comparison of two different modes of schooling (communal vs. child-centered) while other variables remain constant.
The widely recognized decline in educational achievement and increase in inequality in France resulting from individualistic pedagogical theories.
The period of American education in the 1940s and 1950s characterized by high achievement and equality relative to other nations, prior to the major test score decline.
concepts.common_alternative: Educational historians often deny the existence of any 'golden age' of American schooling.
concepts.source_passage: So let’s call the higher-scoring era before the decline—the 1940s and 1950s—a “silver age.”

concepts.author_definition: An approach focused on socialization and the systematic acquisition of shared knowledge, language, and conventions of the public sphere.
concepts.source_passage: Early education should be chiefly communal—focused on gaining proficiency in the language and the conventions of the public sphere.

concepts.author_definition: A label the author argues is used as a pretext to withhold academic content from disadvantaged children, despite such content being harmless to advantaged children.
concepts.common_alternative: The pedagogical belief that certain concepts cannot be taught before a child reaches a specific natural biological stage.
concepts.source_passage: if an advantaged child at age seven knows certain things without harm, then it cannot be inherently harmful or 'developmentally inappropriate' for a disadvantaged child also to know those very same things at the same age.

concepts.author_definition: An educational approach that emphasizes building socialization and shared conventions (language, knowledge, vocabulary) necessary for participation in the public sphere, often through shared topics rather than individualized ones.
concepts.common_alternative: Often caricatured as 'lock-step' or 'factory model' schooling.
concepts.source_passage: Elementary school is a time for building socialization as the only means through which individuality can ultimately express itself. Children need to master the shared conventions of the standard language and of social interaction.

concepts.author_definition: A faith-based paradigm assuming that nature is a manifestation of a benign God, implying that a child's natural development is inherently optimal and should be the primary guide for education.
The educational goal of imparting general, content-independent skills like critical thinking or problem solving, which the author considers a failed and problematic strategy.
The specific shared knowledge and language proficiency that allows an individual to participate effectively in a democracy's public commons.
The idea that the primary goal of education is to impart content-independent abilities like critical thinking or problem solving that can be applied to any subject.
concepts.common_alternative: Often treated as transferable cognitive tools.
concepts.source_passage: the goal of education is the imparting of general skills like critical thinking, creative thinking, problem solving, and cooperative thinking.

concepts.author_definition: A state of performance where form and content, knowledge and skill, are inherently fused and cannot be easily dissociated in the mind.
concepts.source_passage: expertise implies an inherent fusion of form and content, knowledge and skill that can’t easily be dissociated in the human mind.

concepts.author_definition: The principle that skills are intrinsically tied to particular content domains and cannot be applied generally across different subject matters.
concepts.source_passage: Modern cognitive psychology holds that the skills that are to be imparted to a child by the school are intrinsically tied to particular content domains. This is called the domain specificity of skills.

concepts.author_definition: A state of American education characterized by soul-deadening, repetitive exercises aimed at developing nonexistent general skills, which ultimately fails to improve scores or abilities.
concepts.source_passage: A benign child-centeredness coupled with a faulty theory about general skills has led us to a child purgatory of skill drills.

concepts.author_definition: The body of shared, taken-for-granted knowledge that enables a national language to be deployed effectively among strangers.
The ability to write and speak effectively to strangers within the wider community as a mark of educational success and autonomy.
The root meaning of 'development,' implying that child growth is a natural process of internal purposes manifesting in the world.
The shared conventions of vocabulary, spelling, and pronunciation used in the public sphere of the grown-up world.
The overarching idea that all children in a democracy deserve access to shared, enabling knowledge regardless of their home culture.

cases.name: France pre-1989
cases.description: A period where France maintained a highly uniform national curriculum based on the egalitarian principles of the Revolution, ensuring all children, regardless of background, received the same instruction.
key_passages: For many decades the French elementary school had been the pride and the terror of the young, with every child, rich or poor, having to undergo the very same rigors under the same national curriculum.
The egalitarian impulse of this uniformity was expressed early in the Revolution by Condorcet... and re-expressed in the nineteenth century by Jules Ferry.

cases.name: France post-1989 (Loi Jospin)
cases.description: The passage of a law that radically decentralized French education, replacing the national curriculum with local 'projets' and shifting focus to individual interests and general skills.
key_passages: France passed a radical new education law—the loi Jospin—requiring all elementary schools to cease teaching the national curriculum and begin teaching locally determined curriculums.
The new law reflected those ideas: more attention was to be paid to the individuality of each student, to his or her native abilities, interests, and home culture.

cases.name: The French Experiment
cases.description: France adopted American-style child-centered, individualistic educational theories and subsequently suffered a massive decline in student achievement, paralleling the American experience.
key_passages: When the French later adopted those same ideas they suffered a decline of similar massive proportions. Our “Nation at Risk” of 1983 became their “Crise de l’école” of 2007.
The French have now repeated our experiment in educational individualism in a more concentrated and better-documented form.
contestable_aspects: The causal link between specific ideological shifts and the French test score decline.

cases.name: US Education 1940s-1950s
cases.description: A period labeled by Hirsch as the 'silver age' where US schools performed high internationally in both excellence and equity before the decline.
key_passages: As late as the 1940s and 1950s the public education of the United States, for all its racial and social shortcomings, scored near the top among nations in both achievement and equality.
So let’s call the higher-scoring era before the decline—the 1940s and 1950s—a “silver age.”
contestable_aspects: Whether this era was actually superior in terms of equity given segregation.

cases.name: Finland and Japan
cases.description: Nations cited for having schools that successfully accommodate individual students within a framework of shared curriculum topics, rather than creating separate curricula for each child.
key_passages: ...accommodating shared curriculum topics for each child as the best schools do in the community-centered schools of Finland and Japan...

cases.name: The French Post-1989 Reform
cases.description: France adopted American-style individualistic and naturalistic educational ideas, leading to a decline in student outcomes that mirrored the earlier American decline.
key_passages: After 1989, the French in effect duplicated the American decline of the 1960s and 1970s by means of the same basic change in guiding ideas.

cases.name: The US Skill-Drill Purgatory
cases.description: In contemporary American schools, particularly in language arts, a 'benign' focus on the individual child combined with the 'mirage' of general skills has resulted in a curriculum dominated by repetitive drills. These drills are intended to build general thinking skills but result in neither actual skill proficiency nor success on standardized tests.
key_passages: It has induced an ever-more-desperate effort to gain nonexistent skills through soul-deadening drills.

A benign child-centeredness coupled with a faulty theory about general skills has led us to a child purgatory of skill drills. These have produced neither good skills nor good scores on the ever-looming tests.
contestable_aspects: The characterization of skill instruction as 'soul-deadening drills' vs. necessary practice.
The causal link between child-centeredness and the rise of drills.

cases.name: PISA Top Performers (Korea, Japan, Canada, Finland)
cases.description: International comparison of problem-solving abilities showing that nations with communal, content-set curricula produce students who perform significantly better than American students on creative problem-solving tests.
key_passages: The most recent PISA scores for the problem-solving abilities of fifteen-year-olds place American students well below Korea, Japan, Canada, and Finland, nations with set, communal curricula in early grades
contestable_aspects: Whether the success of these nations is due to curriculum or other socio-cultural factors.

thinkers.name: Max Planck
thinkers.key_idea: Scientific paradigms change not through persuasion but through the death of older generations and the rise of the young.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Planck to explain why educational ideas have been so resistant to change and why he is now looking to a younger generation of scholars for a 'new hearing.'
key_passages: He complained that professors never change their minds; they die off, and the younger generations take their places.

thinkers.name: Jules Ferry
thinkers.key_idea: Teachers should impart knowledge that is common and indispensable to all citizens.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Ferry as a historical founder of the egalitarian French system that prioritized communal knowledge.
key_passages: Ferry urged them to teach “that knowledge which is common to all and indispensable to all.”

thinkers.name: Philippe Nemo
thinkers.key_idea: The decline of the French school system under the Fifth Republic.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Nemo as one of the authors who traced the silent intellectual preparation for the 1989 reform through decades of teacher training.
key_passages: Although the structural change was sudden, its intellectual underpinnings had been gradually solidifying since the 1960s, a process that is traced in two key books: Philippe Nemo...

thinkers.name: Liliane Lurçat
thinkers.key_idea: The destruction of elementary teaching and the role of progressive thinkers in that process.
thinkers.source_work: La Destruction de l’Enseignement Élémentaire et Ses Penseurs (1998)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Lurçat's work to support his claim that teacher indoctrination preceded the 1989 law.
key_passages: ...a process that is traced in two key books: Philippe Nemo... and Liliane Lurçat

thinkers.name: John Bishop
thinkers.key_idea: The academic gap between Black and White students in the US was narrowing steadily until recent decades.
thinkers.source_work: Is the Test Score Decline Responsible for the Productivity Growth Decline? (1989)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bishop's work to establish that the mid-20th century was moving toward greater equality before the decline.
key_passages: Moreover, as John Bishop has long pointed out, the education gap between blacks and whites had narrowed steadily until recent decades.

thinkers.name: G. H. Mead
thinkers.key_idea: Individuality is a product of social membership and shared interaction.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Mead to support the theoretical claim that communal socialization is the precursor to, rather than the enemy of, individuality.
key_passages: Only full membership in the tribe leads to individuality, as G. H. Mead profoundly observed.

thinkers.name: Diane Ravitch
thinkers.key_idea: Early American schools, despite lacking a national curriculum, shared a 'common spirit' and communal purpose in teaching history and culture.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Ravitch to support his claim that the U.S. once had a more communal educational approach that has since been lost.
key_passages: The Americans, of course, never had a national curriculum like the French, but the schools of most American districts did in earlier days have a strong communal purposiveness.

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: The scientific attitude of mind as a unifying factor for individualized curriculum.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch credits Dewey for logically identifying the need for a unifying principle when curriculum is fragmented by individualism, but argues that Dewey's solution—the 'habit of thought'—was based on incorrect science about skill development.
key_passages: Unless these steps in advance are to end in distraction, some clue of unity, some principle that makes for simplification, must be found... the needed steadying and centralizing factor is found in adopting as the end of endeavor that attitude of mind, that habit of thought, which we call scientific.
Dewey is right about the structure of the difficulty... but research on thinking skills is now a well-developed field, and its findings are fatal to this crucial refuge.

thinkers.name: K. Anders Ericsson
thinkers.key_idea: The rejection of general cognitive abilities in favor of domain-specific expertise.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Ericsson's work on expertise to provide the scientific foundation for his attack on 'skill-centrism.' He adopts Ericsson's framing of expertise as the proper goal of education.
key_passages: The Cambridge compendium from which the passage is taken is not called A Handbook of Skills... It’s called a Handbook of Expertise, implying that the basis of skills is specific domain knowledge.

actors.name: Lionel Jospin
actors.context: Author of the 1989 French education law that decentralized the curriculum.

actors.name: Giscard d’Estaing
actors.context: Former President of France who supported the uniform national curriculum as an element of social unity.

actors.name: French education schools
actors.context: Accused of indoctrinating teachers with American-style progressive education for two decades prior to 1989.

actors.name: Lionel Jospin
actors.context: French education minister/politician behind the 1989 loi Jospin reform.

actors.name: French Ministry of Education
actors.context: Conducted the longitudinal studies that Hirsch cites as evidence for the failure of the 1989 reforms.

actors.name: Educational Historians
actors.context: Historians who dismiss the idea of a 'golden age' of American education.

actors.name: Reagan Administration
actors.context: Convened the national commission resulting in A Nation at Risk.

actors.name: Knowledge Matters
actors.context: A new advocacy organization launched by experts to promote knowledge-based schooling.

actors.name: Core Knowledge Foundation
actors.context: Founded by Hirsch in 1986 to produce the Core Knowledge Sequence, a content-specific curriculum guide.

actors.name: United States Education System
actors.context: Characterized as having abandoned communal purpose in favor of naturalism and individualism.

actors.name: American school mission statements
actors.context: Cited as evidence of the dominance of the individualism doctrine.

actors.name: The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance
actors.context: Cited as a definitive summary of findings on expertise and the nature of skills.

actors.name: American Policy Makers
actors.context: Accused of using the inaccurate term 'skill' instead of 'expertise,' thus perpetuating a misunderstanding of how children learn.

actors.name: OECD / PISA
actors.context: Source of comparative data on student problem-solving skills used to support the knowledge-based curriculum argument.

objections_raised.objection: Hirsch has not changed his core ideas in thirty years.
objections_raised.response: The basic themes remain the same (social justice/equality), but new cognitive science has added clarity and depth, and the focus has shifted more decisively toward community-centeredness.

objections_raised.objection: The decline in French test scores is primarily caused by an influx of North African immigrants.
objections_raised.response: The decline occurred steeply among all groups, including children of white-collar executives and native-born professionals, not just immigrants.

objections_raised.objection: There was never a 'golden age' of American education.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch concedes there was no golden age but argues data shows a 'silver age' in the 40s/50s followed by a definitive, massive decline between 1960 and 1980.
Socialization and mastery of shared conventions are the only means through which true individuality can eventually express itself.

objections_raised.objection: Individualism and naturalism are necessary for empathetic, loving, and child-centered teaching.
objections_raised.response: Loving and empathetic teaching is indeed the best pedagogy, but it is not dependent on naturalism or individualism; uniform curricula can be equally child-happy.

objections_raised.objection: Individualized, child-centered education leads to a fragmented and chaotic student experience ('in mass').
objections_raised.response: Dewey attempted to solve this using 'critical thinking' as a unifying skill. Hirsch argues this solution fails because general skills don't exist; unity should instead come from shared domain knowledge.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: As I explain in chapter 7, this quick account is deliberately overschematized, since the Americanization process had been going on silently in teacher training for more than two decades.
I will use the details of the French results throughout this book, and devote chapter 7 to an analysis of the French experience.
Evidence of the massive test score decline across all demographics is detailed in chapter 7.
Discussion of 'standard language' is found in chapter 6.
But I will show in chapter 1 that, paradoxically, it is the naturalistic and individualistic view that has turned schools into soulless test-prep factories...
The overwhelming evidence of a massive test score decline at all grade levels in all demographic groups between 1960 and 1980 is found in chapter 7.
In chapter 8 I suggest that in the United States there be at least three different grade-by-grade sequences to choose from...
The overwhelming evidence of a massive test score decline at all grade levels in all demographic groups between 1960 and 1980 is found in chapter 7.
In chapter 8 I suggest that in the United States there be at least three different grade-by-grade sequences to choose from.
Those distracting tests will be the subject of my first chapter.
I explain in chapter 7, this quick account is deliberately overschematized... Americanization process had been going on silently in teacher training for more than two decades.
